sections.heading: Background: The Semi-Pelagian Controversy
sections.html:
Background: The Semi-Pelagian Controversy

The Second Council of Orange (529) was a provincial synod, not an ecumenical council, convened by Caesarius of Arles in southern Gaul. Though local in scope, its canons on grace and free will were confirmed by Pope Boniface II and became authoritative for Western Christendom. For Reformed and Lutheran theology they are especially important, representing the pre-Reformation church's affirmation of sovereign grace against any form of Pelagianism.

The controversy the council addressed was Semi-Pelagianism — a position associated with John Cassian and the monks of southern Gaul. Against Pelagius (who denied original sin and held that men can choose God unaided), and against Augustine (who taught that grace is sovereign and irresistible), the Semi-Pelagians held a middle position: original sin has weakened but not incapacitated the human will; man can take the first step toward God and initiate faith, after which God's grace cooperates with and completes salvation.

Caesarius of Arles, drawing heavily on Augustine, assembled the council to rule definitively on the question. The resulting canons reject any notion that man can initiate or cooperate with grace on the basis of his own natural powers.

sections.heading: The Canons on Grace and Free Will
sections.html:
The Canons on Grace and Free Will

The council issued twenty-five canons and a concluding definition. Selected canons:

Canon 1. If anyone says that not the whole man, that is, both body and soul, was "changed for the worse" through the offense of Adam's sin, but believes that the freedom of the soul remains unimpaired and that only the body is subject to corruption, he is deceived by the error of Pelagius and contradicts the scripture which says, "The soul that sins shall die." (Ezek. 18:20; Rom. 5:12)

Canon 3. If anyone says that the grace of God can be conferred as a result of human prayer, but that it is not grace itself which makes us pray to God, he contradicts the prophet Isaiah, or the Apostle who says the same thing: "I was found by those who did not seek me; I appeared to those who did not ask for me." (Isa. 65:1; Rom. 10:20)

Canon 4. If anyone maintains that God awaits our will to be cleansed from sin, but does not confess that even our wish to be cleansed comes to us through the infusion and working of the Holy Spirit, he resists the Holy Spirit himself who says through Solomon, "The will is prepared by the Lord," and the salutary word of the Apostle: "For God is at work in you, both to will and to work for his good pleasure." (Prov. 8:35 LXX; Phil. 2:13)

Canon 5. If anyone says that not only the increase of faith but also its beginning and the very desire for faith, by which we believe in Him who justifies the ungodly and comes to the regeneration of holy baptism — if anyone says that this belongs to us by nature and not by a gift of grace, that is, by the inspiration of the Holy Spirit amending our will and turning it from unbelief to faith and from godlessness to godliness, it is proof that he is opposed to the teaching of the Apostles. (Phil. 1:29; Eph. 2:8)

Canon 6. If anyone says that mercy is divinely conferred upon us when we believe, will, desire, strive, labour, pray, watch, study, seek, ask, or knock, but does not confess that it is through the infusion and inspiration of the Holy Spirit within us that we have the faith, the will, or the strength to do all these things as we ought; and if anyone makes the assistance of grace dependent on the humility or obedience of man and does not agree that it is a gift of grace itself that we are obedient and humble, he contradicts the Apostle who says, "What have you that you did not receive?" (1 Cor. 4:7; Rom. 9:16)

Canon 7. If anyone affirms that we can form any right opinion or make any right choice which relates to the salvation of eternal life, as is expedient for us, or that we can be saved, that is, assent to the preaching of the gospel through our natural powers without the illumination and inspiration of the Holy Spirit, who makes all men gladly assent to and believe in the truth, he is led astray by a heretical spirit, and does not understand the voice of God who says in the Gospel, "For apart from me you can do nothing," and the word of the Apostle, "Not that we are sufficient of ourselves to claim anything as coming from us; our sufficiency is from God." (John 15:5; 2 Cor. 3:5)

sections.heading: The Concluding Definition
sections.html:
The Concluding Definition

The council's conclusions were summarized in a remarkable definition, among the clearest pre-Reformation statements on sovereign grace:

According to the Catholic faith we also believe this: that after grace has been received through baptism, all the baptized with the help and cooperation of Christ can and ought, if they are willing to labour faithfully, to perform the things that belong to the salvation of the soul. We not only do not believe that any are foreordained to evil by the power of God, but even state with utter abhorrence that if there are those who want to believe so evil a thing, they are anathema. We also believe and confess to our benefit that in every good work it is not we who take the initiative and are then assisted by the mercy of God, but God himself first inspires in us both faith in him and love for him without any previous good works of our own that deserve it. Thus are we made willing to desire baptism; after baptism too, with his help and cooperation, we can do what is pleasing to him.

The definition closes with:

Wherefore, as we have said above, we believe that all good of every kind — whether the beginning of faith, or the increase of good will, or the perseverance unto the end — is a gift of God, not a possession of nature; and that this is most truly asserted, as the holy scriptures and the Fathers teach, who of old wrote against those men who speak of the beginning of salvation as depending on themselves, and not on God.

The canons of Orange do not explicitly address double predestination or perseverance of the saints — questions that would become central in Reformation debates. But their insistence that even the first movement of the will toward God is a grace, not a natural capacity, established the Augustinian framework that the Reformers inherited. (Rom. 9:16; John 6:37; Eph. 1:4–6; Titus 3:5)
